claim: There are four spatial channels in each hemifield of the auditory cortex of cats.

result.claim: There are four spatial channels in each hemifield of the auditory cortex of cats.
result.reasoning: The claim states that there are four spatial channels in each hemifield of the auditory cortex of cats. However, evidence from the paper "Location Coding by Opponent Neural Populations in the Auditory Cortex" (Paper ID: 6431384) refutes this. The abstract of this paper explicitly states, "...we explore an opponent-process theory in which sound-source locations are represented by differences in the activity of two broadly tuned channels formed by contra- and ipsilaterally preferring neurons." This directly contradicts the idea of four spatial channels, instead proposing two broadly tuned channels for sound localization in the auditory cortex of cats. The paper discusses location coding in "three fields of cat auditory cortex" and "each field," implying these two channels are relevant to the hemifields.

evidence.text: The Phase Response Curve (PRC) of brain and peripheral oscillators are remarkably similar.

evidence.text: The greatest modulation is shown for near-midline source locations in each field of cat auditory cortex.

evidence.text: The auditory cortex plays a necessary role in sound localization.
evidence.source: Location Coding by Opponent Neural Populations in the Auditory Cortex

evidence.text: Radioactivity concentration was intermediate in neocortex.
